payload.formula_text:
Category Revenue = SUM(line_item_revenue) grouped by product_category
Category Orders = COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) grouped by product_category
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates revenue and order counts by product category to show which categories drive the most business. Helps identify top-performing categories and underperformers that may need attention.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product category with its total revenue and order count
Sort by revenue to find your highest-earning categories
Sort by orders to see which categories have the most purchase activity
Compare revenue per order across categories to understand average ticket size
Low revenue with high orders indicates lower-priced category items
decisions_answered: Which product categories generate the most revenue?
Are there underperforming categories that need attention?
How is revenue distributed across our product catalog?
Which categories should we prioritize for marketing and inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down performance by product category.

The formula aggregates revenue and order counts for each category, giving you a clear view of which parts of your catalog drive the most business.

Reading this: Look for your top categories by revenue. Are they also your most-ordered categories? If a category has high revenue but few orders, it contains premium products. If it has many orders but low revenue, those are your accessible everyday items.

Compare categories to understand balance. Heavy reliance on one category is risky. A diversified catalog with multiple strong categories is more resilient.

This answers: Which categories matter most? Where should we invest in inventory and marketing? And is our catalog balanced or too concentrated?

payload.formula_text: Customer Count by Type = COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) grouped by customer_type
payload.formula_explanation: Counts unique customers and categorizes them by type (new vs returning, guest vs registered, etc.). This shows the composition of your customer base during the selected period.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare customer counts across different types
Longer bars indicate more customers of that type
New vs Returning split shows acquisition vs retention balance
Guest vs Registered shows account creation adoption
Compare proportions to understand your customer portfolio health
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customers are new vs returning?
Are we retaining customers effectively?
How many customers create accounts vs checkout as guests?
Is our customer mix healthy for sustainable growth?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down your customer base by type.

The formula counts unique customers and groups them by category - new versus returning, guest versus registered, and other relevant segments.

How to read this: Each bar represents one customer type. Longer bars mean more customers in that category. A healthy business typically shows strong returning customer numbers alongside steady new customer acquisition.

If new customers dominate, you may have retention issues. If returning customers dominate with few new ones, growth may stall. The ideal mix depends on your business stage and goals.

Guest versus registered splits help understand account adoption. More registered customers enable better marketing and personalization.

This answers: What does our customer portfolio look like? Are we balancing acquisition with retention? And how can we optimize our customer mix?

payload.formula_text: Hourly Orders = COUNT(orders) grouped by day_of_week and hour_of_day
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates order counts by the day of week and hour of day they were placed. This reveals when customers are most likely to complete purchases.
how_to_read: Rows represent days of the week (Sunday through Saturday)
Columns represent hours of the day (0-23)
Darker cells indicate higher order volume at that time
Lighter cells show lower activity periods
Look for patterns to identify peak shopping times
decisions_answered: When do customers place the most orders?
Are there specific day/hour combinations that peak?
When should we schedule marketing communications?
What hours need the most customer support coverage?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals when your customers prefer to place orders.

The formula counts orders and groups them by day of week and hour of day. Each cell represents a specific time slot - for example, Tuesday at 2 PM.

Darker blue cells indicate high order volume. Lighter cells show quieter periods. Most e-commerce sites see patterns - often higher activity during lunch hours, evening browsing, and weekend shopping sprees.

Understanding these patterns is valuable. Schedule promotions and email sends to coincide with peak shopping times. Ensure customer support coverage matches demand. Plan maintenance or updates during quiet periods.

This chart answers: When do customers shop most? What days and hours drive the most orders? And how should we allocate resources throughout the week?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed each day over the selected time period. This shows the volume of order activity and helps identify trends, seasonality, and anomalies.
how_to_read: The line shows daily order volume over time
Upward trends indicate growing demand or successful promotions
Spikes often correlate with marketing campaigns, sales events, or seasonal peaks
Dips may indicate inventory issues, website problems, or natural demand fluctuations
Compare weekday vs weekend patterns to understand customer shopping behavior
decisions_answered: Is order volume growing or declining over time?
What days or periods see the highest order activity?
Are there seasonal patterns in our order volume?
Did a recent promotion or event impact order counts?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your daily order volume over time.

The formula is simple: count all orders placed each day. Each point represents one day's total order count.

To read this chart: Rising trends mean more orders are coming in. Falling trends need investigation. Look for patterns - do weekends differ from weekdays? Do certain months consistently outperform others?

Spikes usually indicate successful campaigns or seasonal demand. Sudden drops might signal technical issues, inventory problems, or market changes.

This answers key questions: Is our business growing? When do customers prefer to shop? And how did our recent initiatives impact order volume?

payload.formula_explanation: Sums the total revenue from all orders placed each day. Revenue is calculated from order totals including products, shipping, and taxes minus any discounts applied.
how_to_read: The area chart shows cumulative daily revenue over time
Higher peaks indicate strong revenue days
The filled area helps visualize total revenue accumulation
Compare revenue trends to order trends - divergence may indicate AOV changes
Sudden spikes often correlate with high-value orders or promotions
decisions_answered: Is revenue growing in line with our business goals?
What days generate the most revenue?
Are there revenue patterns that differ from order volume patterns?
How does revenue fluctuate throughout the selected period?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes your daily revenue over the selected time period.

The formula sums all order totals for each day. This includes product prices, shipping, and taxes, minus discounts.

Reading this chart: Higher areas mean more revenue that day. The filled area helps you see cumulative performance at a glance. Compare this to your orders trend - if revenue grows faster than orders, your average order value is increasing.

Watch for patterns. Weekends might differ from weekdays. Certain months may consistently outperform. Large spikes often indicate high-value orders or successful promotions.

This chart answers: Is revenue meeting targets? What days perform best? And how does revenue relate to order volume?

payload.formula_text:
Seasonal Index = (Current Week Sales / Average Weekly Sales)
Peak Detection = Weeks where Seasonal Index > threshold
payload.formula_explanation: Identifies products entering their peak selling periods by comparing current week sales to historical averages. A seasonal index above 1.0 means above-average demand. Products with high indices and peak week flags need immediate inventory attention.
how_to_read: Products are listed with their current seasonal index
Index above 1.2 indicates significantly above-average demand
Index below 0.8 indicates below-average demand
Peak week indicator shows products at their historical high-demand period
Recommended actions suggest inventory and marketing responses
decisions_answered: Which products are entering peak demand periods?
Do we have adequate inventory for seasonal surges?
Which products need immediate restocking attention?
What seasonal patterns should we prepare for?
payload.narration_text:
The seasonal alerts table flags products entering peak selling periods.

The formula calculates a seasonal index by comparing current week sales to historical weekly averages. An index of 1.0 means normal demand. Above 1.2 signals a seasonal surge. Below 0.8 indicates a slow period.

The peak week flag marks products at their historically highest-demand week of the year. These need immediate attention to ensure stock availability.

The recommended action column suggests what to do - restock, increase marketing spend, or prepare for the rush.

This answers critical operational questions: Which products are about to surge? Do we have enough inventory? And what actions should we take right now to capitalize on seasonal demand?

payload.formula_text: Status Count = COUNT(orders) grouped by order_status
payload.formula_explanation: Counts orders in each status category (processing, complete, canceled, etc.). This provides a snapshot of your current order pipeline health.
how_to_read: Each slice represents one order status category
Larger slices indicate more orders in that status
Green typically indicates completed/healthy statuses
Red or orange may indicate issues requiring attention
The legend shows exact counts for each status
decisions_answered: How many orders are currently in each stage?
Is our fulfillment pipeline healthy?
Are there bottlenecks causing orders to pile up?
What percentage of orders get canceled or refunded?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows the distribution of orders across different status categories.

The formula simply counts orders in each status - processing, complete, shipped, canceled, and so on.

Each colored slice represents one status. Larger slices mean more orders in that category. A healthy distribution typically shows most orders in completed or shipped status, with minimal cancellations or stuck orders.

Watch for warning signs. A large processing segment might indicate fulfillment bottlenecks. Growing cancellation rates need investigation. Pending orders that stay pending too long affect customer satisfaction.

The legend provides exact counts so you can track specific numbers, not just proportions.

This answers: Where are our orders in the pipeline? Are there operational issues causing delays? And what percentage of orders complete successfully versus getting canceled?

payload.formula_text: Product Revenue = SUM(line_item_revenue) grouped by product, sorted DESC, LIMIT 20
payload.formula_explanation: Calculates total revenue contribution for each product by summing all line item revenues, then ranks products by this total. Shows the top 20 revenue generators.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by total revenue generated
Revenue column shows the total sales value for each product
Qty column shows units sold
Orders column shows how many unique orders included this product
Click any row to drill into product-specific analytics
decisions_answered: Which products drive the most revenue?
Are our top sellers generating healthy margins?
How concentrated is revenue across products?
Which products should we prioritize for inventory and marketing?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your top 20 products by total revenue generated.

The formula sums revenue from every line item for each product, then sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows one product with its SKU, total revenue, quantity sold, and number of orders it appeared in. Revenue concentration matters - if your top 5 products drive 80% of revenue, that's both an opportunity and a risk.

High revenue with high quantity means consistent sellers. High revenue with low quantity indicates premium products. Compare orders to quantity - if one order buys many units, you may have wholesale or bulk customers.

Use this to prioritize. Ensure top sellers never go out of stock. Consider bundling top products with slower movers. Focus marketing on proven winners.

This answers: What products matter most to our revenue? How diversified is our product portfolio? And where should we focus inventory and promotional efforts?
